confidence.disclosure: Bayazid (Abu Yazid) al-Bistami is the founder of the 'intoxicated' school of Sufism and is famous for his ecstatic shathiyat — paradoxical sayings spoken in mystical rapture. He left no writings; his life is known only from sayings collected by Sahlaji (11th c.) and from later hagiographies. Birth and death years are traditional. Specific events should be read as devotional anecdotes.

sources: Abu'l-Fadl al-Sahlaji, Kitab al-Nur min Kalimat Abi Tayfur (11th c.)
R.C. Zaehner, Hindu and Muslim Mysticism (1960)
Carl Ernst, Words of Ecstasy in Sufism (1985)

journey.event: Born in the small town of Bistam in north-eastern Iran (modern Bastam, near Shahrud). His grandfather Surushan was a Zoroastrian who converted to Islam; the family is among the early Persian Muslims of the region.

Receives his early education in the Quran and the religious sciences in Bistam. His mother is, in his own later sayings, the central figure of his moral upbringing — he reportedly spends years in the spiritual practice of serving her.

Travels through Khurasan and beyond, studying with — by Attar's count — 113 spiritual masters across many years. The most famous is the Sindhi (or Indian) teacher Abu Ali al-Sindhi, who, tradition says, learned the doctrine of fana from Bayazid in exchange for instruction in Quranic recitation.
Returns to Bistam and settles there for the rest of his life. He becomes famous across Khurasan for his ecstatic states and his refusal to accept formal disciples or a teaching circle.

Pronounces the famous shathiyat that will scandalise the orthodox and inspire later Sufis: 'Glory be to me, how great is my majesty!' and 'Beneath my cloak there is nothing but God.' These ecstatic sayings, made in states of fana, become the centrepiece of the 'intoxicated' Sufi tradition.

Is reportedly expelled from Bistam several times by religious authorities scandalised by his utterances, but each time the townspeople call him back. He lives in poverty and refuses gifts from the powerful.

Spends his last years in seclusion. His sayings, transmitted by his nephew and a small circle of relatives, will be collected a century later by Sahlaji and form one of the foundational texts of Persian Sufism.
Dies in Bistam and is buried there. His shrine in Bastam is enlarged in the 13th century by the Mongol Ilkhan Ghazan Khan and survives today as one of the great Sufi sanctuaries of Iran.
